dominantFailure: T4 enforcement requires independent monitoring and extraction limits — 5 Near-SOC schemes fail only on T4, not on commons nature or livelihood potential. Infrastructure-only schemes (Dam Safety, Flood Management) lack livelihood components and fall outside SOC scope. The critical gap is absence of mandatory water extraction limits and independent monitoring for all Near-SOC irrigation schemes.

structuralFindings.dominantProblem: Water Commons Can Achieve SOC with Governance Formalization — the resource is inherently commons but T4 enforcement is structurally absent in 5 of 7 eligible schemes
structuralFindings.socException: Jal Jeevan Mission (4/4) and Water User Group Formalization (4/4) achieve SOC through formal governance and statutory sustainability mandates — JJM guidelines create T4 compliance that irrigation schemes lack
crossCuttingPriorities: Universal Water User Group Formalization: Formalize WUGs for 100% of irrigation command areas (currently ~50% coverage); register all VWSCs under Cooperative Societies Act with legal bank accounts by 2027

Mandatory Water Extraction Monitoring: Install flow meters on all irrigation sources (canals/tubewells/tanks); real-time extraction data to WUG committees; halt drilling permits when aquifer shows stress

O&M Fund Earmarking for All Water Infrastructure: 10-15% of capital cost as mandatory O&M fund at project handover; held in WUG/community cooperative account; annual performance audit condition for fund release

Livelihood Integration in Infrastructure Schemes: All irrigation infrastructure schemes (AIBP, PMKSY, Minor Irrigation) must include mandated Delta-Y ≥₹5,000/HH/year target and post-implementation income tracking
contextualFactors: Water Resources Department manages schemes with highest structural SOC potential (64% SOC-adjacent rate) — water is fundamentally a commons resource and governance naturally organizes around community institutions
Chhattisgarh's 4,000+ reservoirs and extensive tank network provide ready commons infrastructure; the primary gap is formalization of governance and T4 sustainability rules

JJM represents the strongest SOC model in the department: universal access + community governance (women-led VWSCs) + livelihood benefits + statutory sustainability monitoring — provides replicable blueprint
T4 enforcement gap is the single most impactful bottleneck: if resolved across all 5 Near-SOC schemes, the department could achieve 64% SOC rate (up from 18%) with ₹44-64 Cr investment

schemes.name: AIBP (Accelerated Irrigation Benefits Program)
schemes.objective: Accelerate completion of ongoing major and medium irrigation projects to expand cultivated area, improve water availability, and support agricultural productivity in command areas.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (public water reserves — surface irrigation commons); T1.2=1 (community irrigation infrastructure with formal access right); T1.3~0.80 (≥75% on commons infrastructure); T1.4=2 (non-excludable benefit)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.80 (command area villages primary beneficiaries); T2.2~0.45 (SC/ST farming households); T2.3=1 (Water User Associations as governance structure)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (irrigation productivity mandated as outcome); Delta-Y: Rs.6,000+/HH/year (rainfed→irrigated: ₹8,000/ha incremental); T3.3=2 (multiple cropping enables pathway diversity); T3.4=1 (government infrastructure as institutional offtake)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (no extraction limits; E/R undefined in project design); T4.2: no water balance assessment mandated; T4.3=0 (WUGs lack independent monitoring capacity and mandate); T4.4=0 (no O&M fund earmarking); T4.5=0 (no replenishment obligation)

upgradePathway: Water Balance Assessment: Every AIBP project requires pre-construction water balance assessment determining sustainable extraction ceiling; published in project DPR
Water User Group Monitoring: Quarterly WUG audits; real-time extraction tracking (flow meters mandatory); red-flag alerts when 80% of sustainable limit approached
Extraction Limits and Pricing: Volumetric water pricing above sustainable extraction limit (incentive to conserve); revenue to WUG O&M fund
O&M Fund Earmarking: 12-15% of AIBP capital deposited to WUG O&M fund at project handover; annual release conditional on extraction monitoring compliance

schemes.name: PM Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY) — Integrated Water Management
schemes.objective: Promote efficient water management through precision irrigation (drip, sprinkler), water harvesting, and conjunctive surface+groundwater use, improving water productivity and reducing extraction pressure.

evidence.t1: T1.1=B (water commons: surface+groundwater shared resource); T1.2=1 (community water infrastructure); T1.3~0.75 (≥70% on commons harvesting/storage); T1.4=1 (partial non-excludability)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.75 (village-level beneficiaries); T2.2~0.40 (SC/ST farmers ≥40%); T2.3=1 (Water Committees governance mandated)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (water-efficient crop income mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.8,000+/HH/year (vegetables/horticulture with precision irrigation); T3.3=2 (high-value crops + year-round production); T3.4=0 (market-driven; no institutional procurement)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (no groundwater extraction limits in scheme design); T4.2: no depletion monitoring mandated; T4.3=0 (no O&M framework); T4.4=0 (no replenishment checks); T4.5=0

upgradePathway: Groundwater Extraction Limits: Maintain water table within 5m of baseline using Atal Bhujal Yojana monitoring data; PMKSY subsidy linked to aquifer health compliance
Recharge Monitoring: Quarterly water table checks; halt further subsidy disbursement if aquifer shows >5% decline from baseline
O&M Fund Earmarking: 12% of capital to Water Committee O&M fund; precision irrigation equipment maintenance over 10-year lifecycle
Replenishment Obligation: In-situ water harvesting on each beneficiary farm (farm pond or recharge pit) mandatory before precision irrigation subsidy released

schemes.name: Minor Irrigation Development (Tank Systems)
schemes.objective: Develop decentralized small-scale irrigation through farm ponds, tank systems, and lift irrigation for dispersed rural communities, expanding water access beyond major irrigation networks.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (shared water bodies — tanks/ponds/lift commons); T1.2=1 (community tank governance right); T1.3~0.85 (≥80% on commons infrastructure); T1.4=2 (non-excludable benefit)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.85 (village-level tank users); T2.2~0.50 (SC/ST dominant in tank command areas); T2.3=1 (Tank Committee governance, 15-30 members)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (tank irrigation productivity mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.5,000+/HH/year (tank irrigation enables 2 crops/year); T3.3=2 (multiple cropping seasons); T3.4=1 (cooperative marketing via Tank Committee)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (no sustainable tank capacity limits; siltation unmonitored); T4.2: no desiltation schedule; T4.3=0 (no O&M fund mandated); T4.4=0; T4.5=0 (tank capacity declines ~1-2% annually through siltation)

upgradePathway: Desiltation Schedule: Mandatory desiltation every 10-15 years as project design requirement; pre-funded from O&M fund established at project completion
O&M Fund Earmarking: 15% of construction cost to Tank Committee O&M fund at project handover; sufficient for 15-year maintenance cycle (desiltation, embankment, sluice repair)
Recharge Monitoring: Quarterly water level checks using staff gauge; Tank Committee reports to Gram Panchayat; annual siltation survey condition for next cycle capital
Community Monitoring: Tank Committee conducts annual siltation survey using standardized protocol; results lodged in district irrigation MIS; triggers automatic maintenance grant if siltation >5%

schemes.name: Groundwater Assessment & Management
schemes.objective: Map and monitor groundwater resources, assess aquifer status, and implement science-based groundwater management to prevent over-exploitation and ensure long-term availability.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (groundwater aquifer commons at aquifer level); T1.2=1 (commons assessment infrastructure); T1.3~0.85 (majority on commons mapping/monitoring); T1.4=2 (non-excludable at aquifer level)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.70 (block-level coverage); T2.2~0.45 (data publicly available); T2.3=0 (assessment outputs to government decisions, not community governance)
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (assessment provides information, not livelihood pathway); Delta-Y: 0 (no direct income from groundwater assessment); T3.3=0; T3.4=0
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (not applicable — assessment is foundational tool, not enforcement); T4.2=0 (monitoring is system output, not enforcement); T4.3=0 (no extraction limits imposed from assessments); T4.4=0; T4.5=0

upgradePathway: Note: Groundwater Assessment is a FOUNDATIONAL GOVERNANCE TOOL; falls outside direct SOC livelihood scheme scope. Should couple with Atal Bhujal Yojana for complete groundwater governance.
Community Data Access: Assessment data made available to Water User Committees in real-time; community dashboard in Gram Panchayat office
Extraction Limit Enforcement: Assessment data directly triggers Atal Bhujal Yojana extraction restrictions in over-exploited blocks; automatic enforcement trigger
Aquifer Recharge Planning: Assessment outputs used to design mandatory recharge structures in PMKSY and AIBP projects; integrated groundwater planning

schemes.name: Dam Safety Program
schemes.objective: Conduct safety audits, rehabilitation, and maintenance of state dams to prevent catastrophic failures, protect downstream communities, and ensure ongoing water storage reliability.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (water storage commons — dam infrastructure); T1.2=1 (commons protection); T1.3~0.90 (≥90% on commons maintenance); T1.4=2 (infrastructure protection benefits all downstream)
evidence.t2: T2.1: all downstream (not targeted community); T2.2: no SC/ST targeting; T2.3=0 (no participatory governance; engineering-driven decision-making)
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (output: infrastructure safety; does not create income); Delta-Y: 0 (no livelihood mandate); T3.3=0; T3.4=0
evidence.t4: T4.1=1 (maintenance framework embedded in dam safety regulations); T4.2=1 (regular safety audits + repair cycles); T4.3=2 (routine assessments per Dam Safety Act); T4.4=1 (ongoing maintenance commitment legally mandated); T4.5=1 (permanent state fund allocation for dam safety)

upgradePathway: Note: This is an INFRASTRUCTURE PROTECTION SCHEME; falls outside direct SOC scope. It is a prerequisite for water commons functioning, not a SOC-eligible livelihood scheme.
Community Downstream Governance: Establish downstream community water committees that participate in dam release scheduling decisions
Livelihood Convergence: Dam safety budget includes community benefit monitoring; link dam rehabilitation to WUG formation in command area
Environmental Flow: Dam safety audits include environmental flow adequacy assessment; protect downstream commons ecology

schemes.name: Flood Management Program
schemes.objective: Implement flood control and management through embankments, drainage systems, and flood-resistant infrastructure to protect communities and agricultural land from seasonal flooding.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (flood commons management — shared river basins); T1.2=1 (commons infrastructure protection); T1.3~0.80 (majority on commons structures); T1.4=2 (flood protection non-excludable)
evidence.t2: T2.1: all communities in flood zone (not targeted); T2.2: no SC/ST priority; T2.3=0 (no community governance)
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (output: flood protection — prevents loss, does not create income); Delta-Y: 0 (no livelihood mandate); T3.3=0; T3.4=0
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (management is reactive — no proactive ecosystem restoration mandate); T4.2: no sustainability obligation; T4.3=0 (no asset monitoring); T4.4=0; T4.5=0

upgradePathway: Note: This is an INFRASTRUCTURE PROTECTION SCHEME; falls outside direct SOC scope.
Community Flood Governance: Village-level flood management committees (part of Gram Panchayat Disaster Committee) with decision-making role in embankment maintenance
Nature-Based Solutions: 20% of flood management budget to natural flood retention (wetland restoration, floodplain commons) — both T1 commons and T4 replenishment
Livelihood Integration: Seasonal employment in flood management maintenance through MGNREGS-NRM; converts protection scheme to income-generating commons work

schemes.name: DRIP (Dam Rehabilitation Integration Program)
schemes.objective: Rehabilitate aging dam infrastructure with World Bank support, improving safety, water storage capacity, and command area irrigation benefit delivery.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (water storage commons — shared dam+irrigation system); T1.2=1 (community irrigation right); T1.3~0.80 (≥75% on commons infrastructure rehabilitation); T1.4=2 (non-excludable benefit)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.80 (command area villages); T2.2~0.45 (SC/ST beneficiaries in command area); T2.3=1 (Water User Group governance mandate in World Bank conditions)
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (infrastructure rehabilitation — not livelihood outcome mandated); Delta-Y: indirect (improved water availability); T3.3=0 (no direct income mandate); T3.4=0 (no livelihood offtake mechanism)
evidence.t4: T4.1=1 (post-rehabilitation O&M systems embedded as World Bank condition); T4.2=1 (dam safety framework; annual assessments); T4.3=2 (safety audits as World Bank safeguard); T4.4=1 (ongoing maintenance commitment in loan covenant); T4.5=1 (O&M budget allocation as WB condition)

upgradePathway: Link DRIP Rehabilitation to Income Targets: Every DRIP dam must demonstrate post-rehabilitation Delta-Y ≥₹5,000/HH/year in command area within 3 years; tracked as project outcome
Livelihood Monitoring: Post-implementation income survey at 1, 3, and 5 years; results published; underperforming dams receive additional WUG support
Benefit-Sharing Mechanism: SC/ST water allocation priority written into WUG constitution at project initiation; no reduction below baseline SC/ST command area allocation
Ecosystem Services Valuation: Include fisheries commons valuation in DRIP project DPR; reservoir fisheries potential managed through mandatory FUG formation

schemes.name: Reservoir Tracker (Real-Time Monitoring System)
schemes.objective: Deploy real-time dam level monitoring, weather forecasting, and water management decision support systems to optimize reservoir operation and improve flood/drought preparedness.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (water commons monitoring — information commons); T1.2=1 (public monitoring infrastructure); T1.3~0.85 (majority on commons monitoring); T1.4=2 (monitoring data is non-excludable public good)
evidence.t2: T2.1: broad public benefit (not targeted community); T2.2: no SC/ST priority; T2.3=0 (benefits primarily government water managers, not community governance)
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (output: monitoring data — not livelihood); Delta-Y: 0 (no direct income); T3.3=0; T3.4=0
evidence.t4: T4.1=1 (real-time monitoring enables adaptive management); T4.2=1 (early warning systems embedded); T4.3=3 (continuous real-time monitoring); T4.4=1 (decision support capability for releases); T4.5=1 (system maintenance budget)

upgradePathway: Note: This is an ENABLING INFRASTRUCTURE TOOL; falls outside direct SOC scope. Foundational technology for T4 enforcement across all water schemes.
Community Access: Reservoir Tracker data made accessible to all WUGs and Tank Committees; simple dashboard for non-technical users
Community Alerts: Automated SMS alerts to WUG committee members when reservoir reaches 80% or 20% capacity; community-level preparedness
Fisheries Integration: Integrate fish biomass sensors into Reservoir Tracker; FUG committees receive real-time stock health data for sustainable harvest decisions

schemes.name: Atal Bhujal Yojana (Groundwater Commons Management)
schemes.objective: Implement sustainable groundwater management through extraction limits, water user committees, and incentive-based conservation, preventing aquifer depletion while ensuring livelihood access.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (groundwater aquifer commons); T1.2=1 (community management right formalized through ABYY); T1.3~0.75 (≥70% on commons groundwater management infrastructure); T1.4=2 (non-excludable benefit)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.75 (block-level groundwater communities); T2.2~0.45 (SC/ST farmers prioritized for new well permits); T2.3=1 (Water User Committee governance — Atal Jal model)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (sustainable groundwater irrigation income mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.5,000+/HH/year (from sustainable groundwater irrigation); T3.3=2 (water-efficient crops + ABYY incentive programs); T3.4=1 (government conservation incentive offtake)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (framework includes limits E/R ≤1.2 but enforcement is weak — advisory not mandatory); T4.2: no binding mandatory extraction limits with enforcement; T4.3=0 (no O&M fund for monitoring infrastructure); T4.4=0 (limited monitoring independence; district officials not independent); T4.5=0 (no penalty enforcement structure for excess extraction)

upgradePathway: Mandatory Extraction Monitoring: Flow meters on all irrigation points in ABYY blocks; real-time data transmitted to Water User Committees; independent district water auditor reviews quarterly
Enforcement with Penalties: Withhold agricultural subsidies for farmers exceeding block extraction quota; restrict new bore well permits when aquifer shows >10% decline from baseline
O&M Fund Earmarking: 10% of ABYY budget to monitoring infrastructure maintenance; community-managed monitoring equipment fund
Independent Monitoring: Block-level water auditors (independent of Irrigation Department); Water User Committee has authority to seal excess extraction points with district endorsement

schemes.name: Jal Jeevan Mission (Rural Water Supply)
schemes.objective: Provide household tap water connections to rural communities from reliable water sources, ensuring year-round safe drinking water access and reducing water-fetching burden on women.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (village water commons — shared source+distribution infrastructure); T1.2=1 (universal community access right mandated by JJM); T1.3~0.85 (≥80% on commons water systems); T1.4=2 (non-excludable: universal household access requirement)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.95 (universal village coverage); T2.2~0.60 (all households including SC/ST; women-centered design); T2.3=1 (Community Water Committee/VWSC mandated with 50%+ women)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (livelihood benefit through time savings+health+diversification explicit in JJM design); Delta-Y: Rs.6,500+/HH/year (time savings ₹2,500+health ₹1,500+diversification ₹3,000); T3.3=3 (time/health/enterprise pathways); T3.4=1 (government procurement for water infrastructure)
evidence.t4: T4.1=1 (source sustainability assessment mandatory per JJM guidelines); T4.2=1 (annual water balance required at source level); T4.3=2 (quarterly monitoring + 6-month quality tests by independent agency); T4.4=1 (fund tranche withheld if quality/sustainability compromised); T4.5=1 (O&M fund 5% of capital per year; held in VWSC account)

upgradePathway: Enhance Livelihood Components: Link JJM time savings to women's skills training (BIHAN SHG integration); 6 months post-connection, women enrolled in micro-enterprise incubation
Community Water Sources Formalization: Integrate JJM with tank/pond management; VWSC also governs community water body (dual commons governance mandate)
Climate Resilience: Embed water harvesting and groundwater recharge targets in each VWSC management plan; 10% of JJM budget to source sustainability investment
Real-Time IoT Monitoring: Upgrade T4.3 further: IoT sensors at tap stands and source; real-time data to VWSC + GP + district; predictive maintenance alerts

schemes.name: Water User Group Formalization (Governance Framework)
schemes.objective: Establish and institutionalize formal Water User Groups for irrigation commons management, embedding community decision-making, benefit-sharing, and sustainability enforcement in water governance.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (irrigation water commons — shared canal/tank systems); T1.2=1 (WUG governance structure formalizes community right); T1.3~0.90 (≥85% on WUG institutional capacity building); T1.4=2 (non-excludable benefit to all command area farmers)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.90 (command area beneficiaries); T2.2~0.50 (SC/ST prioritized in WUG leadership per guidelines); T2.3=1 (elected WUG committees with mandatory SC/ST representation)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (formalized irrigation enables ≥₹8,000/ha/year productivity); Delta-Y: Rs.6,000+/HH/year (from sustainable irrigation access); T3.3=2 (multiple cropping seasons + cooperative marketing); T3.4=1 (collective marketing reduces transaction costs)
evidence.t4: T4.1=1 (water balance assessment mandated per irrigation guidelines); T4.2=1 (annual water allocation per member; proportional distribution system); T4.3=2 (quarterly audits + real-time extraction tracking in advanced WUGs); T4.4=1 (reduced allocation if limits exceeded; subsidy withholding); T4.5=1 (WUG account for O&M fund management)

upgradePathway: Universal WUG Coverage: Formalize WUGs for all 500+ command areas in Chhattisgarh by 2028; prioritize SC/ST-majority command areas for first phase
Women-Centered WUGs: 50% women leadership mandate for all WUGs; women-only sub-committees for water allocation governance in socially conservative blocks
Intersectional Governance: Link WUGs with Gram Panchayat, SC/ST associations, and BIHAN SHGs; integrate water governance with livelihood governance at village level
Real-Time Extraction Dashboard: IoT flow meters in all WUG command areas; real-time data visible to community dashboard + district water office; automated alerts at 80% sustainable limit
